verses.commentary: Paul addresses those familiar with legal principles. Law binds only the living. Death severs legal obligations. This sets up the marriage analogy.
Death breaks the marriage bond. The wife is legally free when her husband dies.
Death changes the legal status. She is free to remarry without guilt.
Now we belong to the risen Christ. The purpose: to bear fruit for God. The law could not produce fruit, but union with Christ does.
The law aroused sinful passions (it revealed and provoked sin). The result was fruit for death. The law exposed sin but could not overcome it.
Service shifts from external code to internal Spirit. The Spirit writes God's law on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33).
Paul uses the tenth commandment as an example. The law exposes internal sin (coveting), not just external acts.
The commandment provokes rebellion. Apart from law, sin lies dormant (dead does not mean non-existent but inactive).
The commandment awakened consciousness of sin. Sin sprang to life, and spiritual death resulted. The law brings awareness of death, not life.
But because of sin, the law became an instrument of death. The law is good, but we are not.
Deception echoes Eden (Genesis 3:13). Sin turns God's good gift (the law) into a weapon against us.
The problem is not the law but sin. The law reflects God's character: holy, righteous, good. It is a perfect standard.

Sin's perversity is exposed by using something good (the law) to kill. The law magnifies sin's sinfulness. Sin becomes utterly sinful (kath' hyperbolēn, καθ' ὑπερβολήν, beyond measure) when it turns God's law against us.
But I am fleshly (sarkinos, σάρκινος), dominated by the flesh. Sold as a slave to sin depicts total bondage. The conflict is between the spiritual law and the fleshly self.
The will is divided. Wanting good but doing evil reveals moral inability. This is the anguish of knowing what is right but being powerless to do it.
The law's verdict is confirmed by my own judgment. The problem is not the law's standard but my inability to meet it.

The true self desires good; indwelling sin produces evil. There is a distinction between the regenerate self and remaining sin. This is the tension of already-not yet: justified but not yet glorified.
The will desires good, but the power to perform is absent. This is moral impotence.
The repetition intensifies the cry. The pattern is persistent: desiring good, doing evil. Keep on doing (present tense) indicates ongoing struggle.
This is not denial of responsibility but recognition of the internal enemy. The battle is within.
il. Evil is right there with me (parakeimai, παράκειμαι, lying beside). The proximity of evil is constant.
ner man) is the regenerate self. Delight in God's law shows a transformed heart. This is Psalm 1:2 lived out. The regenerate person loves God's law.
War language (antistrateuomai, ἀντιστρατεύομαι) depicts intense conflict. Prisoner status shows the power of indwelling sin.

Wretched (talaipōros, ταλαίπωρος) means miserable, afflicted. The body of death (tou sōmatos tou thanatou, τοῦ σώματος τοῦ θανάτου) is this mortal body marked by sin and death. The question expresses helplessness and longing for deliverance.

Thanks be to God, who delivers me through Jesus Christ our Lord! The answer: deliverance comes through Jesus Christ. The cry of despair turns to thanksgiving. Christ is the rescuer. So then, I myself in my mind am a slave to God's law, but in my sinful nature a slave to the law of sin. The tension remains until glorification. The mind serves God's law; the flesh still struggles with sin. This is the Christian's present reality: justified, being sanctified, awaiting glorification.
